name: namespaces
context: Organize and isolate Kubernetes resources with Namespaces — multi-tenancy, resource quotas, and access control
info: Namespaces partition a Kubernetes cluster into virtual sub-clusters. They provide scope for resource names (two Deployments can have the same name in different namespaces), boundaries for [[kubernetes/rbac]] policies, and targets for resource quotas and limit ranges. Agents use Namespaces to isolate environments (dev, staging, production), teams, or applications within a shared cluster. This skill covers creating namespaces, setting default namespaces, applying resource quotas and limit ranges, and namespace-scoped operations.
body:
# Namespaces

Namespaces divide a cluster into isolated virtual sub-clusters. Most Kubernetes resources (Pods, Services, Deployments, ConfigMaps) are namespaced — they exist within a single namespace and their names must be unique within it.

## Default Namespaces

| Namespace | Purpose |
|-----------|----------|
| `default` | Where resources go when no namespace is specified |
| `kube-system` | Kubernetes system components (CoreDNS, kube-proxy, metrics-server) |
| `kube-public` | Publicly readable (cluster-info ConfigMap) |
| `kube-node-lease` | Node heartbeat leases |

## Create and Manage

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: production
  labels:
    environment: production
    team: backend
```

```bash
# Create namespace
kubectl create namespace production
kubectl create ns staging

# List namespaces
kubectl get namespaces
kubectl get ns

# Set default namespace for current context
kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=production

# Or use kubens (faster)
kubens production

# Run commands in a specific namespace
kubectl get pods -n production
kubectl get all -n staging

# Get resources across all namespaces
kubectl get pods --all-namespaces
kubectl get pods -A

# Delete namespace (WARNING: deletes ALL resources inside it)
kubectl delete namespace staging
```

## Resource Quotas

Limit total resource consumption per namespace:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: production-quota
  namespace: production
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "10"          # Total CPU requests across all Pods
    requests.memory: 20Gi       # Total memory requests
    limits.cpu: "20"            # Total CPU limits
    limits.memory: 40Gi         # Total memory limits
    pods: "50"                  # Max number of Pods
    services: "20"              # Max number of Services
    persistentvolumeclaims: "10" # Max PVCs
    configmaps: "50"            # Max ConfigMaps
    secrets: "50"               # Max Secrets
```

```bash
# Apply quota
kubectl apply -f resource-quota.yaml

# Check quota usage
kubectl describe resourcequota production-quota -n production
```

When a ResourceQuota is active, every Pod must specify resource requests and limits — otherwise the Pod creation is rejected.

## Limit Ranges

Set default and max/min resource constraints per Pod or Container:

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: production-limits
  namespace: production
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      default:           # applied when not specified
        cpu: 200m
        memory: 256Mi
      defaultRequest:    # applied when requests not specified
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 128Mi
      max:
        cpu: 2000m
        memory: 4Gi
      min:
        cpu: 50m
        memory: 64Mi
    - type: Pod
      max:
        cpu: 4000m
        memory: 8Gi
```

LimitRanges auto-inject defaults and reject Pods that exceed max or fall below min.

## Namespace Patterns

### By Environment
```bash
kubectl create ns development
kubectl create ns staging
kubectl create ns production
```

### By Team
```bash
kubectl create ns team-backend
kubectl create ns team-frontend
kubectl create ns team-data
```

### By Application
```bash
kubectl create ns app-payments
kubectl create ns app-auth
kubectl create ns app-notifications
```

Combine with [[kubernetes/rbac]] to restrict team access to their namespace.

## Cross-Namespace Communication

Pods in different namespaces communicate via fully qualified DNS:

```
http://<service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local
http://api-service.production.svc.cluster.local
```

See [[kubernetes/services#dns]] for full DNS resolution details.

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace |

sections.title: Resource quotas
sections.body: ResourceQuota limits total resource consumption in a namespace: CPU, memory, Pod count, Service count, PVC count. When active, all Pods must specify requests and limits. Check usage with `kubectl describe resourcequota <name> -n <ns>`. Multiple quotas can exist per namespace — all constraints must be satisfied. Use quotas to prevent runaway workloads from exhausting cluster capacity.

sections.title: Limit ranges
sections.body: LimitRange sets per-container and per-Pod default, min, and max resource constraints. Defaults are auto-injected when omitted from Pod specs. Min/max enforce hard boundaries — Pods violating them are rejected. Use LimitRanges alongside ResourceQuotas: LimitRanges control individual Pod sizing, ResourceQuotas control aggregate namespace consumption.

sections.title: Namespace organization patterns
sections.body: Three common patterns: **By environment** (dev/staging/production) — simplest, mirrors deployment pipeline. **By team** — maps to org structure, combine with [[kubernetes/rbac]] for access control. **By application** — strong isolation between services. Hybrid approaches work too (e.g., team-backend-production). Label namespaces for policy enforcement with NetworkPolicy, PodSecurityStandards, and admission controllers.

links.context: Services are namespaced — cross-namespace access uses FQDN

related.note: RBAC RoleBindings are namespace-scoped — use with namespaces for multi-tenancy
Cross-namespace service communication uses FQDN DNS

scripts.name: Setup Namespace
scripts.description: Create a namespace with resource quota and limit range
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
NS=${1:?Usage: setup-namespace.sh <namespace> [cpu-quota] [memory-quota] [max-pods]}
CPU=${2:-10}
MEMORY=${3:-20Gi}
MAX_PODS=${4:-50}

echo "Creating namespace: $NS"
kubectl create namespace "$NS" --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -

echo "Applying resource quota..."
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: ${NS}-quota
  namespace: ${NS}
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "${CPU}"
    requests.memory: ${MEMORY}
    pods: "${MAX_PODS}"
EOF

echo "Applying limit range..."
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: ${NS}-limits
  namespace: ${NS}
spec:
  limits:
    - type: Container
      default:
        cpu: 200m
        memory: 256Mi
      defaultRequest:
        cpu: 100m
        memory: 128Mi
EOF

echo ""
echo "Namespace $NS configured:"
kubectl describe resourcequota ${NS}-quota -n "$NS"

frontmatter.author: dojo-community
frontmatter.audience: developers, DevOps engineers, SREs, AI agents
